reasoning:
This is a fascinating problem. Let me think deeply using the "coralline" seed as a conceptual guide.

Coral doesn't save—it *accretes*. Individual polyps deposit tiny, irregular amounts of calcium carbonate whenever conditions allow. No polyp knows when its next deposit will happen. Yet the colony builds cathedrals over time. The structure itself becomes productive habitat, generating value beyond the sum of deposits.

What if retirement wasn't about accumulating currency in an account, but about accreting ownership stakes in an ever-growing productive commons?

```json
{
  "label": "Accretive Reef Economy",
  "design_principles": "Replace 'saving' with 'accreting.' Contributions of any size, at any time, buy permanent stakes in shared productive infrastructure. The reef grows even when you can't contribute.",
  "core_mechanism": "Micro-contributions purchase fractional ownership in a collectively-managed pool of productive real assets (renewable energy, automated services, land trusts). Retirement income flows from production, not drawdown.",
  "how_it_works": "Any surplus (even $1) auto-flows into the 'reef'—a legal trust/DAO hybrid. Funds acquire revenue-generating assets: solar arrays, fulfillment robots, IP portfolios, agricultural land. Your 'balance' is reef-shares, not dollars. In retirement, you receive proportional income from actual asset output. Critical: shares are non-dilutive and permanent once earned. The system needs: (1) legal framework for fractional micro-ownership, (2) asset acquisition governance protocol, (3) automated sweep mechanisms from bank accounts.",
  "what_is_new": "Decouples retirement security from both contribution consistency AND financial market performance. You own production capacity, not financial claims. The commons grows collectively while you rest.",
  "why_it_works": "Irregular earners need a system that doesn't punish gaps. By pooling contributions into real assets, individual variability becomes collective stability. Solar panels generate electricity whether you contributed last month or not. The reef's diversity (many asset types) provides resilience no individual portfolio could achieve.",
  "why_it_fails": "Governance of shared assets is notoriously difficult. Who decides which assets to acquire? How do you prevent capture by sophisticated actors? Collective ownership historically struggles with free-rider problems and decision paralysis. May require unprecedented levels of institutional trust.",
  "medium_term": "Emergence of 'reef cooperatives' competing on asset acquisition strategy. New profession: reef portfolio managers. Regulatory frameworks for fractional infrastructure ownership. Early retirees demonstrating the model works.",
  "long_term_vision": "Retirement security becomes a byproduct of collective infrastructure investment. The distinction between 'saving for retirement' and 'building shared prosperity' dissolves. Communities own their energy, housing, and automated services. Irregular earners have the same security as salaried workers because the reef doesn't care when you fed it—only that you did."
}
```

solution.label: Accretive Reef Economy
solution.design_principles: Replace 'saving' with 'accreting.' Contributions of any size, at any time, buy permanent stakes in shared productive infrastructure. The reef grows even when you can't contribute.
solution.core_mechanism: Micro-contributions purchase fractional ownership in a collectively-managed pool of productive real assets (renewable energy, automated services, land trusts). Retirement income flows from production, not drawdown.
solution.how_it_works: Any surplus (even $1) auto-flows into the 'reef'—a legal trust/DAO hybrid. Funds acquire revenue-generating assets: solar arrays, fulfillment robots, IP portfolios, agricultural land. Your 'balance' is reef-shares, not dollars. In retirement, you receive proportional income from actual asset output. Critical: shares are non-dilutive and permanent once earned. The system needs: (1) legal framework for fractional micro-ownership, (2) asset acquisition governance protocol, (3) automated sweep mechanisms from bank accounts.
solution.what_is_new: Decouples retirement security from both contribution consistency AND financial market performance. You own production capacity, not financial claims. The commons grows collectively while you rest.
solution.why_it_works: Irregular earners need a system that doesn't punish gaps. By pooling contributions into real assets, individual variability becomes collective stability. Solar panels generate electricity whether you contributed last month or not. The reef's diversity (many asset types) provides resilience no individual portfolio could achieve.
solution.why_it_fails: Governance of shared assets is notoriously difficult. Who decides which assets to acquire? How do you prevent capture by sophisticated actors? Collective ownership historically struggles with free-rider problems and decision paralysis. May require unprecedented levels of institutional trust.
solution.medium_term: Emergence of 'reef cooperatives' competing on asset acquisition strategy. New profession: reef portfolio managers. Regulatory frameworks for fractional infrastructure ownership. Early retirees demonstrating the model works.
solution.long_term_vision: Retirement security becomes a byproduct of collective infrastructure investment. The distinction between 'saving for retirement' and 'building shared prosperity' dissolves. Communities own their energy, housing, and automated services. Irregular earners have the same security as salaried workers because the reef doesn't care when you fed it—only that you did.
